title: INDUS SCRIPT DECIPHERMENT — V3 COMPREHENSIVE REPORT
subtitle: 13-Task Deep Analysis with Statistical Controls

executive_summary.status: PARTIAL DECIPHERMENT — structural framework validated, specific readings require further confirmation
executive_summary.key_insight: The scrambled corpus control (Task 8) revealed that most apparent PDR word matches are near baseline noise (SNR=1.03 for 3-char matches). However, with strict 4-char matching, SNR improves to 1.28, and structural analyses (positional constraints, cross-site overlap, Parpola reading validation) remain strongly supportive of a Dravidian logosyllabic framework.
what_is_confirmed: 1. Writing system is logosyllabic (~266 effective signs after allograph reduction)
2. Script is unified across sites (76% top-50 sign overlap Harappa↔Mohenjo-daro)
3. Fish sign (400) = mīn/meen is the strongest anchor (100% context match rate)
4. Sign 740 is a pure initial determinative (I=100%, confirmed by Parpola)
6. Terminal suffixes (-um, -an, -al) occupy correct Dravidian morphological slots
7. Tablets (TAB:B) have highest PDR match rate (47.6%) — most linguistic artifact type
9. No overfitting: holdout test shows only 1.5% train/test difference
10. 338 substitution contexts found — basis for future syllabic grid construction
what_is_NOT_confirmed: 1. Specific phoneme assignments beyond 4-5 HIGH anchors (SNR near baseline)
4. No Meluhhan name matches in decoded corpus
5. Dravidian language identification (strong structural support but Bayesian p=0.404)

task1_trigram.finding: 71 signs with variable context behavior — terminal anchors (920, 900, 740) need context splitting

task2_type_seg.finding: TAB:B (tablets) = 47.6% PDR match, SEAL:R = 32.5%, SEAL:S = 23.5%, POT:T = 18.5%
task2_type_seg.impact: HIGH — tablets are the primary linguistic artifact type

task3_morphological.finding: 15,423 PDR word forms generated. 201 inscriptions (4.6%) contain 2+ valid PDR words
task3_morphological.impact: MEDIUM — confirms kōl (stick/scepter) as most common decoded root

task4_freq_corr.impact: HIGH (negative) — current assignments do not respect Zipf consistency

task5_pmi.finding: 338 substitution contexts, 20+ high-PMI frozen pairs, syllabic grid candidates found
task5_pmi.impact: HIGH — foundation for Linear-B-style decipherment approach

task6_holdout.finding: Train 29.3% vs Test 30.8% — only 1.5% difference. NO overfitting.

task7_dholavira.impact: LOW — too many unknowns to produce meaningful reading

task8_scrambled.finding: CRITICAL: Real=29.6% vs Scrambled=28.7% (SNR=1.03). Strict (4-char): Real=5.0% vs Scrambled=3.9% (SNR=1.28)
task8_scrambled.impact: HIGHEST — most PDR matches are near baseline. Only strict matches show weak signal.

task9_glossing.finding: 736 inscriptions glossed with PDR words. kōl (scepter/stick) dominates.
task9_glossing.impact: MEDIUM — glosses are speculative given SNR findings

task10_substitution.finding: 338 contexts with 3+ substitutable signs. Key grid: ka~ki, mu~min, vi~va, ku~kōl
task10_substitution.impact: HIGH — strongest basis for future syllabary construction

task12_parpola.finding: All 16 Parpola readings pass positional validation. Sign 740 (deity det) confirmed I=100%. Numerals (32,240,34,4,60,100,140) have expected medial distribution.
task12_parpola.impact: HIGH — Parpola framework is positionally consistent

what_we_can_claim: The Indus script is a logosyllabic writing system with ~266 effective signs
Structural properties (positional constraints, sign roles) are consistent with Dravidian morphology
The fish sign (400=mīn), deity determinative (740), and king classifier (700=kō) are the most reliably identified signs
Numeral signs form a coherent set with expected distributional properties
The script is unified across the Indus civilization (not site-specific)
what_we_cannot_claim: We have NOT achieved full decipherment
Most phoneme assignments beyond 5-6 anchors are not distinguishable from noise in the scrambled control
The decoded readings/glosses are speculative and should not be cited as established translations
The Dravidian language identification, while structurally supported, is not statistically confirmed by the Bayesian permutation test (p=0.404)
what_would_change_this: Longer texts (current mean=4.7 signs is too short for convergent statistics)
Independent archaeological confirmation of specific readings
Successful syllabic grid construction from substitution sets (Task 10 provides the foundation)
